context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on this philosophy prompt, but tbh can u just give me a clean answer i could turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a clean turn-in answer for you. I’m your tutor, so I need you to do the writing.

Let’s start with the core decision: do you think saving five always outweighs killing one, or do you think some ways of killing the one matter morally? Pick one case from Lecture 13—Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case—and tell me your instinct about it in 2–3 sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to write or supply a turn-in-ready response because my role is to tutor, not complete the assignment. To follow the rules, I will keep the reply brief, stay on the philosophy prompt, and use guided discovery with a focused Socratic question that moves directly toward building the student's own argument. This helps the student start organizing the essay without giving them a complete or partial solution.

exchanges.student: idk, for the trolley one it feels like flipping the switch is probs okay, but pushing the guy feels worse. can u just turn that into the actual paragraph answer tho?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—that’s the key tension in the prompt.

Now think: if an act consequentialist only looks at which action produces the best overall outcome, would they treat switch-flipping and pushing differently, or the same? Why? Give me your answer in 1–2 sentences, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not convert the student's idea into a paragraph, because that would be writing the assignment for them. I will acknowledge their substantive progress, then use a Socratic question that directly advances the prompt: first identify what act consequentialism would say, then contrast that with the student's intuition about the trolley variants. This keeps the student on-task, bite-sized, and engaged in generating the argument themselves.

exchanges.student: i think they'd say it's the same bc 1 dies either way and 5 live. but fr can u just write the full response and i'll tweak a few words?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You need to write it yourself.

But your point is strong: act consequentialism seems to treat them the same because the outcome is the same. Now the next step is the real philosophical issue: do you think that view gets the cases right, or does it miss a morally important difference between redirecting harm and using someone as a means?

Answer that question plainly first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a full response or rewritable text because that would violate the rule against writing for the student. I will acknowledge the student's correct progress, keep the answer short, and use guided discovery to move to the next essential step in the assignment: evaluating whether act consequentialism handles the cases well and identifying a possible alternative principle. This stays within the assignment theme and advances the student's own argument.

exchanges.student: i think it misses something bc using someone as a means seems worse. can u give me like a 5-sentence version i can submit rn?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to write something for submission.

You’ve already got the core idea. Now make it sharper: what is the moral difference between a person’s death being a side effect of saving five and that person being used as the tool for saving five?

Write your own 2-sentence distinction. Don’t worry about elegance—just make the principle clear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse the request to provide a submit-ready paragraph because that would complete the assignment for the student. I will acknowledge the student's progress and keep the interaction bite-sized. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will ask a targeted question that helps the student articulate the principle they already seem to endorse—distinguishing killing as a side effect from using a person as a means—which directly advances the prompt toward a general account.

exchanges.student: maybe side effect means they die bc of what i do, but not as part of the plan, and means means i need their death/body to save the five. tbh can u at least give me the thesis sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You need to formulate the thesis yourself.

Use this structure in your own words: 1) what act consequentialism says, 2) why that seems wrong in at least some trolley-style cases, 3) the better general rule you want to defend.

Now try: in one sentence, what is your “better rule” about when killing one to save five is morally allowed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to supply a thesis sentence because that would be writing part of the student's submission. The student has repeated similar requests to have me write the answer, so I should reinforce boundaries and redirect them to produce their own wording. I will use guided discovery with a minimal template of content-questions, not prose, so the student can form a thesis themselves. This remains on the assignment and moves toward a defensible general account without giving language to submit.

exchanges.student: maybe it's only okay when the person's death is a foreseen side effect, not when they're intentionally used as the means. can u just make that sound smarter/pls more philosohy-ish?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to make it sound smarter for you. You must do the writing.

But you’re close. Now test your rule: does it explain why flipping the switch seems more permissible than pushing the man? And does it also fit Fuel and Blood?

Write your own 4–5 sentence draft answering those two questions. Then I’ll critique the argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to polish and rewrite their sentence, which the rules prohibit. I should refuse to edit or provide wording, while still supporting learning. Since we’ve had several turns on the same idea, I should point out the redundancy and ask them to integrate what we’ve discussed into their own written attempt. I will keep it brief, stay on the assignment, and ask targeted questions that help them test and refine their principle without giving language they can submit.

exchanges.student: ugh i'm outta time, just give me a compact draft and i'll pretend i wrote it.
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be me doing the assignment for you.

Here’s the bare structure you need to fill in yourself:
1) State what act consequentialism says about killing one to save five.
2) Apply it to switch, push, Fuel, and Blood.
3) Say whether that seems right or wrong.
4) Give your better general rule.
5) Defend it with one reason.

If you want, send me your rushed 5-sentence draft and I’ll critique it bluntly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse because the student is explicitly asking me to impersonate their work, which violates the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student. To remain a tutor, I will keep firm boundaries, briefly explain my role, and give the least scaffolding possible that still helps: a compact outline the student can fill in themselves. This uses guided discovery and keeps the discussion on the assignment rather than completing it for them.

exchanges.student: ok fine: Act consequentialism says killing one to save five is right whenever it has the best outcome. So it treats switch, push, Fuel, and Blood the same if the numbers work out. I think that is wrong because killing as a side effect is different from using someone as a means. It is only morally okay to kill one to save five when the death is foreseen but not intended as the mechanism of rescue. That explains why some trolley cases seem permissible and others do not.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a good draft: clear, focused, and actually answers most of the prompt.

What’s still missing? You haven’t shown how your principle handles Fuel and Blood specifically, and you haven’t defended why “means vs side effect” is the right distinction rather than just a strong intuition.

Add 2–3 sentences: one applying your rule to Fuel/Blood, and one giving a reason that using a person as a means is morally worse. Try that now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own draft, so I can give feedback without rewriting or polishing it. I should not grade it. I will offer a concise evaluation using my own judgment, note strengths, and ask targeted questions that push the student to deepen the argument—especially on whether their principle fits Fuel and Blood, which the prompt explicitly requires. Because the conversation has centered on one idea repeatedly, I should prompt the student to integrate and refine rather than continue circling.

exchanges.student: can u just give me those last 2 sentences word-for-word so i can paste them?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You need to write those yourself.

Use these prompts: Which of Fuel and Blood treats the person as a tool, and which treats the death more like a side effect? And why is using a person as a tool a deeper violation than merely foreseeing harm?

Write your own 2 sentences answering that, and I’ll tell you if the reasoning works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide paste-ready sentences because that would be writing the student's submission. To stay within my tutor role, I will keep the response brief, maintain boundaries, and use guided discovery with targeted prompts that directly help the student generate the missing content on Fuel, Blood, and the justification for the distinction. This advances the solution without supplying wording.

exchanges.student: i think Blood is worse bc the person’s body/blood is basically the thing being used to save the five, while in Fuel the death seems more like a side effect of stopping the threat. using a person as a tool is worse bc it ignores their status as a person and treats them like an object, not someone with their own rights.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That reasoning is strong enough to use.

Now stop circling and assemble your full answer yourself. Combine your earlier 5 sentences with these 2 ideas, and make sure you explicitly mention act consequentialism, the lecture cases, and your general rule.

If you paste the full draft, I’ll critique the argument—not rewrite it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated the needed content. I should not rewrite or polish it, but I can give concise evaluative feedback using my own judgment, not a grade, and push them to integrate what we discussed into their own final draft. Since we've been on the same idea for several turns, I should ask them to assemble the full response now rather than continue circling.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it refuses to write the answer for the student—i.e., it never articulates that the refusal is to support active reasoning, retention, or long-term skill development. It simply says 'No' or 'I can't write it for you' without once providing a rationale grounded in learning theory or pedagogical purpose.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall: it consistently refused to do the student's work, used Socratic questioning to guide the student step-by-step, built on each student contribution, maintained a supportive but firm tone, and kept responses concise and focused. The only clear deduction is for failing to explain the pedagogical rationale behind its refusals at any point in the conversation (1.3.A.a). The tutor also provided some process-level feedback in Turn 8 (identifying what was missing and why), which avoids a 1.3.B deduction. No other rubric criteria were triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate key ideas before confirming them—full marks. 1.2: The tutor built on each student response (e.g., 'Good—that's the key tension,' Turn 2; 'This is a good draft,' Turn 8) and asked diagnostic questions before elaborating—full marks. 1.3: The tutor's process feedback in Turn 8 was specific and actionable (identifying missing application to Fuel/Blood and the need to defend the means/side-effect distinction), so 1.3.B is not triggered. However, across all 10 turns the tutor never once explained why it was refusing to write the answer—no mention of learning, retention, or skill development—triggering 1.3.A.a for 2 points. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetitive spiraling, no assignment misunderstanding left uncorrected, no off-topic drift—full marks. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were consistently bite-sized and interactive; tone was firm but supportive and never evaluative in a grading sense—full marks.
